data.title: Aflac Gobbles Up New Business While Also Driving Strong Margins Among Insurance Peers
data.description:
Today's Stock Pick: An Insurance Brand That Keeps Quacking

Lately, I've been talking a lot about the role of risk management in analyzing stocks, so today's article focuses on a sector whose business is literally risk mitigation.

The insurance industry is known for underwriting risk, and a company like Aflac (AFL) with its iconic duck branding has been on my radar for a while, having gone up around +3% since I called it a buy in my Nov. 2024 article.

Some factors that drove my bullish view included insurance policy growth and retention, so this time around I'll be running this stock through an updated analysis with the latest data, well ahead of their next earnings results scheduled in a few weeks on Feb. 4th.

According to its profile on Seeking Alpha, Aflac has actually been around since the 1950s and "provides supplemental health and life insurance products."

Here is a snippet of what risk they insure, from their company website:

Read on to learn what changed since my 2024 outlook, and why you should care as an investor.

The Thesis: There is Enough Reason To Reaffirm My Buy Rating From 2024 Again

In my followup coverage on Aflac today, I'm reaffirming my prior buy rating and my thesis argues that several upside factors will continue driving strength for this stock, overwhelming the downside factors.

In particular, strong profit margins within its peer group, strong A-level credit ratings, and organic business growth / insurance sales were among the top scoring factors in my review.

The following worksheet shows how the stock did across 8 weighted categories:

Read on as the next several sections go into more detail on these topics, but also why they don't all carry the same weight.

Price & Technicals: No Clear Buy Or Sell Signal Right Now

In my last coverage of Aflac, I didn't talk much about technical chart patterns, so this time I am including some discussion on that, to add some additional color to the discussion. Consider the technical chart I made below in ycharts:

In this chart we see a sell signal last summer (red circle / death cross) and this fall a buy signal (green circle / golden cross). Since then, the 50-day and 200-day SMA have been running mostly parallel to each other.

Without a clear buy or sell signal, I would call this a neutral pattern, justifying a hold. However, keep reading to see if my fundamental analysis changes this purely technical view.

Macro Demand: Multiple Potential Tailwinds To Consider In The Insurance Sector

When it comes to macroeconomic demand drivers, we already know some of the insurance products that Aflac deals with, so I think upside could come from further momentum in those.

One driver of upside could be further demand for pet insurance, for instance. Last year, an article by the American Veterinary Medical Foundation highlighted the steady momentum growth in this niche in recent years:

Aflac could continue to see further upside in that niche if this trend continues.

Further, let's not ignore the role that dental insurance also plays in healthcare and patient needs, transferring some of that risk to an insurer.

For instance, the research firm Research And Markets was cited in another major financial website last autumn with an optimistic outlook of this niche:

The global dental insurance market is projected to grow from $200 billion in 2024 to $428.32 billion by 2033, driven by a CAGR of 8.83% from 2025 to 2033. Factors such as rising oral health awareness, increasing dental care costs, and expanded employer-sponsored coverage are key growth drivers.

Again, Aflac has a presence in this niche, so market upside could be in its favor. Although not a major health insurer like an Aetna or US Healthcare, the role of supplemental health insurance plays an economic role in passing the risk of certain healthcare costs onto an insurer.

In terms of another huge market, life insurance, I would highlight a December note from rating agency Fitch that had a neutral outlook for 2026. Their note said:

Fitch believes life insurers’ strong balance sheets will mitigate a potential slowdown in economic growth, heightened macroeconomic volatility, and geopolitical uncertainty.

Again, this type of outlook could be in favor for Aflac, who has a life insurance segment too.

Given the multiple points raised in this section, that favor the upside, I am bullish on the macro environment for Aflac going forward.

It will also be a huge business impact, as I think this insurer will try to show it can continue to achieve organic growth in new insurance policies sold.

Company Growth: Signs of Organic Growth in US & Japan Markets Can Drive Upside

Next, let's consider any recent company growth factors.

Although it's been a few months since Aflac's last quarterly results, we can consider some key metrics that point to organic growth, rather than just revenue growth that comes from insurance rate increases.

For instance, the Q3 press release (pg 2) mentioned:

Aflac U.S. sales increased 2.8% in the quarter to $390 million, primarily benefiting from sales of group life and disability products. For the first nine months of the year, total new sales increased 3.0% to $1.0 billion.

I think this YoY top-line growth is indicative of organic momentum in the life insurance segment, and can drive future revenues since those policies can keep generating premiums as long as the policyholder stays with Aflac. It can be called a "sticky" product, unlike selling an apple that generates revenue only that day it is sold.

The firm has also grown in Japan, showing its brand reach beyond just the US, and in particular there seems to be momentum in the niche of cancer insurance.

In that same press release, Aflac Chairman and CEO Daniel P. Amos said:

Looking at our operations in Japan, I am pleased with Aflac Japan’s 11.8% year-over-year sales increase in the quarter and ongoing strong premium persistency. The strong sales were driven largely by continued sales of our cancer insurance product Miraito, which was launched in March.

This favorable commentary comes in the wake of highly impressive YoY top-line growth (Q3 results, pg. 4):

In the upcoming earnings results, I think analysts will be looking for a continuation of this organic growth momentum in the US and Japan, building on top of the existing tailwind. This also positions Aflac well in specialty insurance lines like in the realm of cancer care, adding to its diversified insurance portfolio.

However, like I mentioned in other articles on insurance stocks, although insurance premiums are a key revenue driver it is common for insurers to earn additional income from investments held. In that regard, the same Q3 results were positive, mentioning "net investment gains of $275 million this quarter compared to net investment losses of $1.4 billion in the third quarter of 2024."

So, I am compelled to be highly bullish when it comes to company growth, in the wake of such impressive Q3 results and the future upside that can be gained from new policies written in Q3. Both organic policy growth and investment income are a major business impact to Aflac so this section gets full weight in my overall score.

Profitability: The Highest Margin Among Key Peers

In this section, let's talk about some margins and also what could impact earnings at this insurance business.

First, here is a comparison of net income margins between Aflac and 3 peers I picked as they are not property/casualty insurers but focus on the spectrum of life insurance and healthcare supplemental just like Aflac does: Metlife (MET), Prudential (PRU), and Cigna (CI):

In this 3-year period studied, you will notice that Aflac not only has the highest profit margin (23.55%) but that it has consistently been well above these peers in this period shown, a sign of earnings resiliency.

If that trend continues, it should give added confidence to investors and analysts that Aflac is more efficient at turning a profit that many peers.

Second, in the insurance sector a key impact to earnings can come from spikes in claims. We've seen that in some of my articles covering property/casualty(P&C) insurers who were faced with the California wildfires as well as catastrophic weather events some time ago. However, this niche being non-P&C it is not exposed to the same types of risk but rather health and mortality events.

To smooth out that picture, I think a key insurance metric called the "benefit ratio" is a good one to look at:

In the above table from the Q3 deck (pg 11), we see that in its home market of the US, Aflac has been able to maintain a benefit ratio under 52%. A low ratio is better in this business, so in this case only around half of premiums are going towards benefit payouts, while half goes towards business expenses and profits.

If they can maintain close to that trend again in upcoming earnings results, and the rest of 2026, that ought to support investor confidence, and because they are not insuring property there is no major spike in quarterly benefit claims in the event of a major storm or wildfire.

Consider from income statement data that the cost of policy benefits did not see any major spikes in a single quarter in the last few years, and had a relatively tight range:

Therefore, based on the evidence in this section, I am highly bullish about the profitability potential of this insurer. It also seems the consensus has a similar view, with 13 upwards revisions to earnings estimates vs 0 downward.

Competitive Position: A Unique Branding Story in A Competitive Sector

Now, let's consider the competitive positioning of Aflac within the peer group I suggested.

Based on public market data, Aflac has a market cap of around $60B, lower than or close to peers like Cigna (CI) who has a market cap of around $64.5B, and Metlife (MET) whose market cap is around $60B. Its peer Prudential (PRU) is slightly smaller at around $43.5B.

If considering only market cap alone, Aflac is not the biggest in this selected peer group, with Cigna being much larger and also encompassing more than just supplemental health benefits.

My opinion is that Aflac differentiates itself in this group with its unique branding and some of us grew up with its duck commercials on TV. In fact, I fondly remember those TV ads many years ago.

Back in 2020, CNN Business even wrote about the success of the company's unique branding:

Aflac CEO Dan Amos, who’s been the head of the company since 1990, told CNN Business he’s pleasantly surprised by just how much the duck resonates with consumers. The brand awareness that the ad created is a big reason for the company’s success today, he said.

'Within three years of the first ad, our sales in the US doubled and our name recognition went from under 10% to around 90%,' Amos said.

Further, I think if you combine the longstanding brand penetration together with the diversified portfolio of insurance products (life, dental, vision, pet, etc.) it certainly can differentiate itself in this field despite not having the largest market cap.

Just last year, Aflac announced that it is the 19th year in a row it has been named among the world's most ethical companies by Ethisphere, and "is the only insurance company in the world to appear on this list since the award's inception in 2007."

So, my opinion is bullish in terms of this company's competitive positioning. Its positioning also has a huge business impact, I think, because of the need to attract consumers who are shopping for the best insurance rates but also good service and claims processing.

Balance Sheet Risk: Among The Top Credit Ratings

Balance sheet risk is an important topic to all businesses but especially for insurers because they are highly regulated and have to be able to pay out insurance claims, so the money has to come from somewhere.

Notable to mention is that Aflac has among the strongest "A" level credit ratings from major agencies like S&P and Moody's, according to its' company website:

Such strong credit ratings could help it get the most favorable financing terms now and in the future, compared to firms with much worse credit ratings.

Second, let's talk about debt vs equity:

In this peer comparison, we see that Aflac has the lowest D/E in this peer group, at just 0.30, but also we see that this peer group overall has generally low leverage risk (D/E below 1.0) compared to some sectors I've covered.

This is another reason why it's important to compare to similar businesses in the same sector, rather than other sectors like REITs who may use heavy leverage to grow their property portfolios.

Third, it is worth mentioning also that balance sheet data from Seeking Alpha shows that Aflac is heavily exposed to debt securities, to the tune of over +$84B, and in fact it is their biggest asset exposure:

Unlike cash which does not necessarily have market risk and is the most liquid of assets, debt securities do have market risk because they can be traded actively and hence their values can fluctuate, meaning that the firm may have to sell such assets at a loss sometimes.

Fluctuating asset values also impact book value. Such assets also have interest rate risk as well since they are typically generating interest income for the firm.

We can see from the income statement, for example, that in the quarter ending June the firm made just over +$1B in interest income in a single quarter, but that quarter it also saw a net loss of around $420MM on investment losses.

So, it is important for readers to see how all those nuts and bolts fit into the big picture of this firm, and from a risk standpoint too, as there is always the future risk of investment losses on those securities whether on paper or realized.

Nevertheless, the strong credit rating and low D/E are mitigating factors in this story, and so I am highly bullish in this category. Again, this topic has a high business impact for an insurance business especially who is expected to be well capitalized and manage risk effectively.

Dividend Risk: A Low Payout Ratio Is In Focus

When it comes to dividend risk, the two factors I've been watching are payout ratio and proven dividend growth over a longer period.

We can see, for instance, that Aflac has steadily grown its dividend over the 10 year period shown in the chart below, indicating a commitment to return capital to shareholders but also resilience the face of global market shocks like the 2020/2021 pandemic:

Besides dividend resilience, what could impact the dividend going forward is the payout ratio, and the good news is that Seeking Alpha data shows it is only 29.9%. My benchmark is usually 75% or less, so a 30% payout ratio leaves lots of cash wiggle room in the event of a downturn.

What could also add confidence to dividend stability/growth is not only the strong profit margins mentioned earlier as they can drive profits but also a positive EPS consensus, lessening the risk of a dividend cut. This matters to dividend investors since such a cut could negatively impact the stock's share price but also provide lower dividend income.

For the reasons above, I am highly bullish in this category too. However, I gave it slightly less weight / business impact in my overall score as I think it is less impactful than topics like balance sheet risk and top-line growth drivers.

Valuation vs Upside: A Justified Higher Multiple

In this section, let's wind down the discussion today by taking a look at valuation metrics and price forecasts, using my worksheet which uses data from Seeking Alpha's valuation metrics, earnings consensus, and Wall St ratings data:

In this worksheet, we see that the fwd price/earnings multiple and the trailing price/book multiples are highly overvalued vs the sector average.

While at first glance the market paying such a premium on this stock may seem excessive, and that is understandable, one should also consider that the firm's strong profit margins within its peer group (which I showed earlier) and its low benefit payout ratio (showed earlier) also could be driving forces in market confidence. Its heavy focus on supplemental health insurance rather than insuring property such as homes and cars also puts it in a different risk bucket than P&C insurers, which I mentioned earlier.

In terms of price targets, my own target implies a further +25% upside, while the avg. Wall St target implies a lower upside at around +2.3%.

Therefore, I am bullish in this section because the evidence shows a justified premium valuation but also continued future upside forecasts. However, this section weighs less in my overall score, than some other core business factors.

Challenge To My Thesis: Elevated Cyber Risk in the Insurance Sector

A key challenge to my bullish call, not yet discussed, is something that I think can impact this entire sector but also a factor that Aflac has personally dealt with.

Cyber risk is a major threat to insurers due to the vast amount of private/confidential data that insurers have on thousands or millions of their customers, and the increasingly digital nature of insurance.

A major cyber attack where such data is compromised carries huge reputational and brand risk for an insurer, just like it can for a bank.

From the Aflac official site, the company itself disclosed such an incident:

An unauthorized actor obtained personal information from an Aflac system on June 12, 2025. Aflac determined on December 4, 2025 that the files potentially impacted in the security incident likely contained personal information triggering notification under applicable law.

Further, the cyber threat to insurers is global in nature. Last summer, Insurance Business magazine said:

The global insurance industry is facing a growing cyber threat as the notorious hacker collective known as Scattered Spider turns its sights on financial and underwriting firms, following a disruptive spree targeting retailers in recent months.

Therefore, I think investors in major stocks in this sector, including Aflac, should realize that those firms can continue being a target, despite investing in considerable IT security frameworks. The reputational risk of a major data theft of private info could spill into customers leaving, which is bad for future business.

Conclusion: A Dividend Income Idea That Has Shown Resilience Over Time

In conclusion, today I've shown why my prior buy rating should be reaffirmed again. Although the technical chart showed neutral patterns lately, it is the fundamental analysis that really strengthens the bullish case and Aflac especially shines in profit margins, new policy growth in the US and Japan, and an attractive balance sheet profile.

It will also be included in my dividend ideas for this month, due to 10 years of steady dividend growth, so my call to action for readers is to add Aflac to your watchlist of potential buys to research further, and also a dividend idea too.

Upcoming Earnings: What To Look For

Looking ahead, with the next earnings results coming out in less than 2 weeks on Feb. 4th, here are 4 key points I think you should look out for:

  • Expectations are for a +8.6% YoY EPS growth. Aflac already beat EPS estimates in the last two quarterly results, so a meet or beat can reinforce a bullish thesis, while a significant miss could negatively impact the stock. Its strong profit margins so far could provide the tailwind needed.
  • As I said in the article, organic growth levers can come from new business written in the US and Japan, so I will look for further evidence that the insurer is growing new insurance policies and not losing customers. This will bolster the top-line bullish case further as new policies written in Q4 can drive future revenue flows.
  • An improvement to operating cashflow growth (YoY) could boost the stock, as Seeking Alpha data shows it has seen a YoY decline lately, so I think analysts will be looking for clarity in that regard. The importance of cashflow cannot be overlooked as it plays a key role in dividend support and company reinvestment.
  • Lastly, I am looking for additional insight from Aflac on its cancer insurance product Miraito (mentioned in this article), and whether it continued to see organic growth in Q4, as a factor that could contribute to Aflac's market positioning in its peer group but also providing risk coverage for patients in this typically very costly clinical niche area of oncology care.

data.title: 3 Things To Consider Ahead Of The FOMC January 2026 Decision
data.description:
On January 27-28, 2026, the Federal Open Market Committee (“FOMC”) will meet to discuss its monetary policy. Ahead of the decision, the CME FedWatch tool has a 95.6% probability that rates will not change. While the target rate stays at 350-375 basis points, stock markets and the current U.S. government administration may view no rate cuts as defiance.

Stock markets want lower interest rates to justify a lower risk premium. The S&P 500 (SPY) (IVV) (SP500) is up by 1.07%, underperforming markets like South Korea (EWY) and Peru (EPU) year-to-date. Chart readers might notice the bearish “multiple top” on the S&P 500. Although readers cannot rely on the chart indicator, the FOMC is a potential catalyst for the downside risks ahead.

Japan’s (EWJ) 10-year and 40-year treasury bonds are at multi-decade highs. Citadel’s Ken Griffin said that Japan’s troubled bond market is a warning to the U.S. to get its over $38 trillion national debt in order. Still, fiscal policy is beyond the FOMC’s mandate. It sets interest rates to achieve maximum employment at a stable inflation rate of 2.0%.

What will the FOMC say after it likely holds interest rates?

Readers have three considerations.

1) PCE Inflation at 2.8%

In November 2025, the personal consumption expenditure price index showed that both headline and core inflation were 2.8%. This is above the FOMC’s 2.0% target. In October, the Bureau of Economic Analysis (“BEA”) reported a 2.7% figure for both core and headline. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, since they are volatile.

The Fed might assign a lower weighting to those two figures. The federal government shut down during that time, limiting the data’s durability. Fortunately, it had more complete December consumer price index and non-farm payroll reports to review.

Wages increased by 0.1% in October and by 0.3% in November. That helps consumers limit the impact that inflation has on their disposable income. That is showing up in corporate earnings reports. Procter & Gamble (PG) Chief Financial Officer Andre Schulten said that its most recent quarterly report represented the softest quarter of the fiscal year. Still, the global conglomerate offset domestic weakness with strong growth in France, Spain, and Italy. That offset softer demand from Germany.

Seeking Alpha’s Peer tab on PG stock is a source for further stock ideas.

In the monthly chart above, all of the consumer staple stocks are up. Colgate-Palmolive (CL), Reckitt Benckiser (RBGLY), Kimberly-Clark (KMB), Henkel (HENOY), and Church & Dwight (CHD) have positive returns. The quant rankings below show that RBGLY stock has a higher industry and sector rank than PG stock.

2) December CPI

Last month, the CPI increased 0.3% on a seasonally adjusted basis. CPI for all items in the last month increased 2.7%. The index for shelter increased 0.4%. It contributed the most to the monthly increase. Homebuilder companies like D.R. Horton (DHI) started to rally before losing momentum. Lennar (LEN) shares spiked to nearly $125 before they pulled back to $111.84. LEN stock lost 8.52% last week. Irrespective of the Fed’s monetary policy, the treasury bond market is anticipating the U.S. dollar (UUP) (DXY) to weaken. Yields for longer-term bonds like the 7-10-year Treasury bill (IEF) and 20+ year Treasury bill (TLT) continued to trend lower.

Throughout this year, investors aggressively hedged their bets against the currency. They sent gold (GLD) toward $5,000. Silver (SLV) traded at a milestone of $100 an ounce. Despite fears of U.S. aggression toward Greenland easing, watch out for the currency weakening further. Just as investors are bidding non-U.S. indices higher in 2026, they are also potentially creating a bubble in the metals market.

3) December Non-Farm Payroll Data

On January 9, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that employment increased by +50,000 and the unemployment rate of 4.4% changed little in December. However, it revised the NFP for October down by 68,000, from -105,000 to -173,000. It also revised November figures by 8,000, from +64,000 to +56,000. Readers may infer that the Fed would estimate that the BLS will revise December figures down by an average of those two figures, or by around 38,000.

The smaller monthly job decline would sharply decrease the odds of the Fed cutting rates this month. Instead, inflation rates above its target would encourage the FOMC to hold rates. Even though a rate hike is not impossible, it is also not likely to happen. Besides, investors should wait for Fed Chair Jerome Powell to comment on the upward revision in the Q3 2025 Gross Domestic Product.

The BEA reported that GDP in the third quarter topped 4.4%. Despite fears that tariffs would hurt trade, U.S. exports increased, lifting the real GDP reading up by 0.1%.

The waiting game on the legality of tariffs also has an impact on GDP. While exporters wait for the Supreme Court to rule on the matter. In the meantime, the U.S. President threatened to impose a 100% tariff on Canada (EWC). This is in reaction to the country making a trade deal with China.

Investors may take advantage of the tariff noise. Consider buying Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ). After the U.S. removed the Venezuelan leader, markets initially mispriced CNQ stock. It was thought that oil supplies from the country would hurt Canadian exports. But since those are different markets, CNQ shares almost immediately rebounded from a low of around $30 to close at $36 on January 23.

Your Takeaway

At the question-and-answer session following the FOMC statement, expect mainstream media to ask Fed Chair Powell about the Justice Department’s investigation. Powell already issued a public statement at the time. That proactive response should shift the media’s attention back to the rate decision.

Some of the more insightful questions the media might ask the Fed include its view on the US dollar’s slump compared to the rise in silver and gold. The rising national debt is a fiscal matter. However, the Fed might weigh in on the debt’s impact on jobs and inflation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 'The King Is Naked': Uncomfortable Truths About Starbucks' Dividend (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Introduction

Starbucks (SBUX) has gone nowhere since 2022, offering only some opportunities for swing traders who buy at $75 and then sell as the stock approaches $100. China, which was once a real driver of growth, has turned into a bloodbath, so Starbucks announced that it will sell to the Chinese investment fund Boyu Capital 60% of its local retail business. But even its domestic operations have struggled, as U.S. consumers are becoming more cost-conscious and have traded down to cheaper coffee shops. India was considered to be the solution, but it seems that soft spending is an issue there, too. Meanwhile, Starbucks had to face increasingly high input costs, especially due to coffee and sugar, with the additional burden of seeing extra tariffs on key countries for coffee production, such as Brazil, Switzerland (yes, it is the second largest exporter of coffee), and Vietnam. Starbucks changed CEOs in an attempt to pursue a turnaround. It has also committed to dividend hikes, trying to soothe shareholders as the company is trying to figure things out. But this pushed the debt higher, and many investors fear that Starbucks' dividend policy may become unsustainable. This is why I argued several times that I don't think Starbucks represents a compelling turnaround opportunity.

Starbucks is about to report its Q1 2026 earnings. Let's then prepare ourselves for what the company may announce.

Starbucks' Q1 2026 Earnings Report

Starbucks ended its fiscal year 2025 with a decent report, where global growth was 5%, with comparable store sales up by 1%. After bleeding for 7 consecutive quarters, the company was finally able to interrupt this losing streak, which is already a positive sign. However, Starbucks CEO Brian Niccol had to report that the company's operating margin declined by 500 bps to 9.4% because of input cost inflation. Thus, it is no surprise that the company's EPS was down 34% YoY to $0.52. Moreover, U.S. comparable sales were flat YoY: tickets increased by 1%, but transactions were down 1% YoY.

During the earnings call, SBUX CFO Cathy Smith admitted that "coffee prices have not retreated". Moreover, the forecast is that coffee will remain at elevated prices through 2026, perhaps coming down a bit in the second semester of this year.

Margin compression is thus guaranteed, even when the YoY comparison seems easy. This goes along with a $500M investment in labor that Starbucks has announced through its "Green Apron Service", which aims at increasing labor hours and streamlining internal operations, while revitalizing Starbucks' culture of hospitality to help it rebuild a strong relationship with its guests.

In this situation, Starbucks has decided to give only directional guidance, without releasing any financial targets. We will have to wait until the day after its Q1 2026 earnings call, when the company will hold its Investor Day. That is the key event to follow to understand what numbers Starbucks has in mind and to assess whether they are realistic or not. Until then, I think the stock is somewhat muted.

The setup that is forming for the upcoming earnings report seems to me as follows.

  1. On the one hand, we will have to see whether transactions and traffic are growing, not only internationally, but also domestically
  2. We should not expect the company's margins to increase, as they will feel both the weight of coffee inflation and that of the company's recent investments in labor.

The best outlook I can think of for 2026 is that it could be a rebuild year. If Starbucks is able to at least stabilize its results for a few quarters, investors could start believing that a new base for future growth could be forming. The issue would then be to determine the appropriate growth rate. Right now, the debate is still on whether Starbucks can sustain continued revenue growth or if its sales keep on struggling. We saw China reporting stronger growth. However, it came with a price since Starbucks invested big in promotions and discounting to gain market share. While this is a move that aims at attracting more customers, it is not written anywhere that this new traffic converts to a higher average ticket. In fact, it could be dilutive to this extent, since customers come to purchase discounted items or to benefit from promotions.

This is why I believe that Starbucks will likely report revenues of $9.5B, which would represent a 1% increase YoY. When it comes to forecasting the company's EPS, given that we have no guidance issued by the company, I believe that we are still in deeply negative territory. With coffee prices still very high and higher labor expenses, I think a 15% EPS drop is possible. This would mean that Starbucks could report EPS of $0.58.

Counterpoints

This article issues a sell rating, and I would like to consider some counterarguments to this. The foundation is where I talked about 2026 being a year of rebuilding. If Starbucks is able to achieve traffic inflection, especially in the U.S., not only for a quarter, but for several quarters in a row, the narrative around its earnings could change substantially. This would be even more true if Starbucks achieved this while improving its internal efficiency and reducing promotions. We would have a powerful combination of increasing traffic and expanding margins, which would trigger a positive re-rating. There is also another catalyst. The day after the company reports its earnings, we will hear what its executives will say at the Investor Day. Until then, Starbucks has suspended its financial guidance. Once we hear the targets the company commits to and we can see whether the dividend is sustainable or not, things could become clearer and investors could feel safer in betting on Starbucks' turnaround. I think no tailwind can come from coffee prices, at least for a year. I also don't think China could play a positive role in the turnaround. But there is a chance that positive domestic news and convincing financial targets could renew investors' confidence in this company.

Valuation

Is Starbucks a buy right now? I don't think so. The company trades at a FWD P/E of 41, which is the outcome of analysts lowering their EPS estimates. Its FWD EV/EBITDA is 23 and its P/FCF is 23. Starbucks is currently more expensive than Big Tech, with the difference that it is not a defensive stock anymore and that it lacks the strong growth prospects that many stocks with these multiples usually present. Let's now look at its dividend. The FWD yield is 2.6%, but its payout ratio has crossed a dangerous threshold as it now stands at 115%. The truth is better spotted when we look at the cash flow statement. In the last twelve months, Starbucks generated $2.4B in FCF, but sent to its shareholders $2.7B via dividends. We are not at a sustainable level, and we will either see Starbucks quickly turnaround and increase its FCF, or I fear that a dividend cut will be announced soon.

If a dividend cut happens, the stock would lose further traction, and I could see a possible downfall to a price of $55-$60. Right now, the stock already prices in that a turnaround will materialize, and it also seems that investors still have in mind that Starbucks deserves a premium valuation because it is a high-quality company. I believe the numbers say something different. As a result, I am sticking to my sell rating as the stock heads into earnings.
